| FCC | 12 | 12 nearest neighbors | $\alpha + 4\beta[\cos(k_x a/2)\cos(k_y a/2) + \cos(k_y a/2)\cos(k_z a/2) + \cos(k_z a/2)\cos(k_x a/2)]$ | $24\vert\beta\vert$ |

---

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Tight-Binding Band Formation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Isolated Atom with Energy Level E0] --> B[Two Atoms Brought Close Together]
    B --> C[Diatomic Molecule: Energy Level Splits into 2 Levels]
    C --> D[N Atoms Forming Crystal Lattice]
    D --> E[Atomic Levels Broaden into Continuous Energy Band]
    E --> F[Bloch Wavefunction Construction via LCAO]
    F --> G[Secular Equation det H-ES = 0]
    G --> H[Band Dispersion Relation E k Computed]
    H --> I[Bandwidth W = 2Z times absolute Beta]
    H --> J[Band Gap Eg between Valence and Conduction Bands]
    H --> K[Effective Mass m star at Band Edge]
    I --> L[Apply to Conducting Polymer]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Polyacetylene: Pi Band from 2pz Orbitals]
    L --> N[Polyaniline, Polypyrrole: Similar TB Analysis]
    M --> O[Peierls Distortion: Dimerization Opens Gap]
    O --> P[Solitons, Polarons, Bipolarons as Defect States]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Tight-Binding Workflow Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        I1[Crystal Structure Data] --> I2[Atomic Orbital Basis Selection]
        I2 --> I3[Compute On-site Integral alpha]
        I2 --> I4[Compute Transfer Integral beta]
        I2 --> I5[Compute Overlap Integral S]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Stage]
        I3 --> C1[Construct Hamiltonian Matrix H k]
        I4 --> C1
        I5 --> C2[Construct Overlap Matrix S k]
        C1 --> C3[Solve Secular Equation det H k minus E times S k = 0]
        C2 --> C3
        C3 --> C4[Eigenvalues give Energy Bands E n k]
        C3 --> C5[Eigenvectors give Bloch Coefficients]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        C4 --> O1[Band Structure Plot]
        C4 --> O2[Density of States g E]
        C4 --> O3[Effective Mass m star]
        C4 --> O4[Fermi Surface for Metals]
        C5 --> O5[Orbital Character of States]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison: Tight-Binding vs. Nearly Free Electron

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electronic Structure of Crystalline Solids] --> B{Starting Picture}
    B -->|Electrons strongly bound to atoms| C[Tight-Binding Method]
    B -->|Electrons nearly free, weak periodic potential| D[Nearly Free Electron Method]
    C --> E[Basis: Atomic Orbitals phi a centered on R j]
    D --> F[Basis: Plane Waves exp i k dot r]
    E --> G[Bloch Sum: psi k = sum over j of exp i k dot R j times phi a]
    F --> H[Bloch Sum: psi k plus small periodic perturbation]
    G --> I[Result: Narrow Band, Strong Atomic Character]
    H --> J[Result: Broad Parabolic Band, Weak Modulation]
    I --> K[Best For: Inner Shells, d and f Electrons, Organic Polymers]
    J --> L[Best For: Simple Metals like Na, Al, Cu]
```

### 4.4 Physical Schematic Description (for diagrams that cannot be drawn in Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic of a 1D Tight-Binding Chain with $N=8$ atoms:**
>
> Below is a textual description of the canonical 1D tight-binding chain. Picture a horizontal line with 8 equally spaced dots representing atomic positions at $x = 0, a, 2a, 3a, \ldots, 7a$. Each dot is the center of a localized atomic $s$-orbital drawn as a small bell-shaped curve. The vertical position of each orbital is the same (all at energy $E = \alpha$). A horizontal dashed line at $E = \alpha$ represents the on-site energy reference.
>
> When the tight-binding Hamiltonian is "switched on," the sharp levels broaden into a continuous band of width $W = 4\vert\beta\vert$, with the band maximum $E_{max} = \alpha + 2\beta$ at $k=0$ and band minimum $E_{min} = \alpha - 2\beta$ at $k = \pm\pi/a$. The dispersion $E(k) = \alpha + 2\beta \cos(ka)$ is a cosine curve plotted versus $k$ in the first Brillouin zone $[-\pi/a, \pi/a]$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the tight-binding approximation. List the three fundamental integrals that characterize it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **tight-binding approximation (TBA)** is a method for computing the electronic band structure of crystalline solids by constructing Bloch wavefunctions as a **linear combination of atomic orbitals (LCAO)** localized at each atomic site. It is most appropriate when the periodic crystal potential is strong and the electrons are tightly bound to their parent atoms, such as in $d$-band metals, inner shells, and $\pi$-conjugated polymers.

The three fundamental integrals are:

1. **On-site (Coulomb) integral $\alpha$:** $\alpha = \int \phi_a^*(\mathbf{r})\, H\, \phi_a(\mathbf{r})\, d\tau$ — the average energy of an electron in the atomic orbital on a single site.

2. **Transfer (resonance/hopping) integral $\beta$:** $\beta = \int \phi_a^*(\mathbf{r})\, H\, \phi_b(\mathbf{r}-\boldsymbol{\delta})\, d\tau$ — the matrix element that allows an electron to hop between neighboring sites.

3. **Overlap integral $S$:** $S = \int \phi_a^*(\mathbf{r})\, \phi_b(\mathbf{r}-\boldsymbol{\delta})\, d\tau$ — the measure of spatial overlap between orbitals on adjacent sites (often neglected in the simplest TB model).

**Mark allocation:** Definition (1 mark) + three integrals (1.5 marks) + clean presentation (0.5 mark).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Explain the physical significance of the bandwidth $W = 4 \vert \beta \vert$ in a 1D tight-binding chain. How does it relate to electrical conductivity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a 1D monoatomic tight-binding chain with one $s$-orbital per atom, the band dispersion is $E(k) = \alpha + 2\beta \cos(ka)$. The **bandwidth** is defined as the total energy spread of allowed electronic states:

$$W = E_{max} - E_{min} = (\alpha + 2\beta) - (\alpha - 2\beta) = 4 \vert \beta \vert$$

**Physical significance:**

- A **larger** $\vert \beta \vert$ means electrons can **delocalize more easily** between neighboring atoms. The wavefunctions spread out across the entire chain, the band becomes **wide**, and the electrons behave more like **free electrons** with high group velocity $v_g = (1/\hbar)(\partial E/\partial k)$. This implies **high electrical conductivity** (e.g., metals).

- A **smaller** $\vert \beta \vert$ means electrons remain **localized** near their parent atoms. The band is **narrow**, electrons have low mobility, and the material is a **poor conductor or insulator**. In the limit $\beta \to 0$, the band collapses to a set of discrete atomic levels (insulator).

- In **conducting polymers** like polyacetylene, the bandwidth of the $\pi$-band (set by the C–C hopping integral $\beta$) directly determines the carrier mobility and hence the electrical conductivity. Doping or chemical modification that increases $\beta$ (shorter bonds, better orbital overlap) enhances conductivity.

**Mark allocation:** Formula (1 mark) + physical meaning in 2 limits (1.5 marks) + conducting polymer relevance (0.5 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice (you must answer either OR). Standard split: part (a) = 7 marks, part (b) = 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Starting from the linear combination of atomic orbitals (LCAO) ansatz, derive the tight-binding band dispersion relation $E(k) = \alpha + 2\beta \cos(ka)$ for a one-dimensional monoatomic chain. Clearly state all assumptions used.

**(b) [7 marks]** For *trans*-polyacetylene, model the chain as a dimerized 1D tight-binding system with two alternating hopping integrals $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$. Derive the energy bands and show that the system develops a band gap $E_g = 2\vert \beta_1 - \beta_2 \vert$. Comment on the role of the **Peierls distortion** in opening this gap.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of 1D Tight-Binding Dispersion:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Each atom contributes one atomic orbital $\phi(x - ja)$ localized at site $ja$.
2. Orthogonal basis: $S_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$.
3. Only nearest-neighbor hopping: $\beta_{j, j\pm 1} = \beta$; all others are zero.
4. On-site energy $\alpha$ is the same for every atom.
5. The chain has $N$ atoms with periodic boundary conditions.

**LCAO ansatz (1 mark):**

$$\psi_k(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} e^{ikaj}\, \phi(x - ja)$$

The factor $1/\sqrt{N}$ ensures normalization; the phase $e^{ikaj}$ enforces the Bloch condition.

**Schrödinger equation (1 mark):**

$$H\psi_k(x) = E_k \psi_k(x) \quad \text{where} \quad H = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_0}\frac{d^2}{dx^2} + V(x)$$

**Project onto basis orbital $\phi^*(x - ma)$ (2 marks):**

$$\int \phi^*(x-ma)\, H\, \psi_k(x)\, dx = E_k \int \phi^*(x-ma)\, \psi_k(x)\, dx$$

Substituting the LCAO sum and using the assumptions:

$$\alpha\, e^{ikam} + \beta\, e^{ika(m-1)} + \beta\, e^{ika(m+1)} = E_k\, e^{ikam}$$

**Factor out $e^{ikam}$ (1 mark):**

$$\alpha + \beta e^{-ika} + \beta e^{ika} = E_k$$

**Apply Euler's formula to obtain the cosine (1 mark):**

$$E_k = \alpha + 2\beta \cos(ka)$$

This is the required tight-binding dispersion, valid in the first Brillouin zone $k \in [-\pi/a, +\pi/a]$.

---

**Part (b) — Dimerized Chain (Polyacetylene):**

**Set up the $2 \times 2$ Hamiltonian (2 marks):**

With unit cell containing two CH units and alternating hopping integrals $\beta_1$ (short bond) and $\beta_2$ (long bond):

$$H(k) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & \beta_1 + \beta_2 e^{-ika} \\ \beta_1 + \beta_2 e^{ika} & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

(On-site energies set to 0 for simplicity; equal for both atoms.)

**Solve the secular equation (2 marks):**

$$\det[H(k) - EI] = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E^2 = \vert \beta_1 + \beta_2 e^{ika} \vert^2$$

$$E^2 = \beta_1^2 + \beta_2^2 + 2\beta_1\beta_2 \cos(ka)$$

$$\boxed{E_{\pm}(k) = \pm \sqrt{\beta_1^2 + \beta_2^2 + 2\beta_1 \beta_2 \cos(ka)}}$$

**Extract the band gap at the Brillouin zone edge (1.5 marks):**

At $k = \pi/a$, $\cos(\pi) = -1$:

$$E_{\pm}(\pi/a) = \pm \sqrt{\beta_1^2 + \beta_2^2 - 2\beta_1 \beta_2} = \pm \vert \beta_1 - \beta_2 \vert$$

$$E_g = E_+ - E_- = 2 \vert \beta_1 - \beta_2 \vert$$

**Discussion of Peierls distortion (1.5 marks):**

In a 1D metal with a half-filled band (one electron per atom), the system is **unstable** to a **Peierls distortion**: the lattice dimerizes so that the atoms pair up, with alternating short and long bonds. This:
- **Doubles the unit cell**, halving the Brillouin zone.
- **Folds** the band back on itself at the new zone boundary $k = \pi/2a$, opening up a gap.
- The total energy is lowered because the electronic energy gain (filled states below gap drop in energy) outweighs the elastic cost of lattice distortion.
- For $\beta_1 \neq \beta_2$, a finite gap $E_g$ opens, transforming 1D polyacetylene from a hypothetical metal into a **semiconductor** — the central insight behind the existence of conducting polymers as a separate class of materials.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Define the **density of states** $g(E)$ in a tight-binding band. Derive an expression for $g(E)$ in a 1D chain with dispersion $E(k) = \alpha + 2\beta \cos(ka)$ and discuss the physical meaning of the **Van Hove singularities** at the band edges.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain how the tight-binding model is applied to conducting polymers, with specific reference to **polyacetylene**. Discuss the formation of the $\pi$ and $\pi^*$ bands from the carbon $2p_z$ orbitals and explain the origin of the band gap in terms of the **dimerization coordinate** $u = (a_1 - a_2)/2$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Density of States in 1D:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The density of states (DOS) $g(E)\, dE$ is the number of allowed electronic states per unit length (or per unit cell) whose energies lie between $E$ and $E + dE$. It is the key link between the band structure $E(k)$ and experimentally measurable quantities such as specific heat, optical absorption, and the Seebeck coefficient.

**General expression in 1D (1 mark):**

$$g(E) = \frac{1}{\pi} \left\vert \frac{dk}{dE} \right\vert$$

(The factor 2 accounts for the two states at $+k$ and $-k$ in the Brillouin zone, and $1/(2\pi)$ is the $k$-space density of states.)

**Invert the dispersion (1.5 marks):**

$$E - \alpha = 2\beta \cos(ka) \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{1}{a}\arccos\left(\frac{E - \alpha}{2\beta}\right)$$

**Differentiate (1.5 marks):**

$$\frac{dk}{dE} = -\frac{1}{a\sqrt{4\beta^2 - (E - \alpha)^2}}$$

**Final expression (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{g(E) = \frac{1}{\pi a \sqrt{4\beta^2 - (E - \alpha)^2}}}$$

valid for $E \in [\alpha - 2\beta,\, \alpha + 2\beta]$, and $g(E) = 0$ outside the band.

**Van Hove singularities (0.5 mark):** The DOS **diverges** as $(E - E_{edge})^{-1/2}$ at the band edges $E = \alpha \pm 2\beta$. This is a **square-root singularity**, characteristic of 1D systems. Physically, near the band edges, the dispersion $E(k)$ is **flat** ($dE/dk \to 0$), so a large range of $k$-states map to a narrow range of $E$, producing a large $g(E)$. These divergences are observable as sharp peaks in the optical absorption spectra of conducting polymers.

---

**Part (b) — Application to Polyacetylene:**

**$\pi$ and $\pi^*$ band formation (3 marks):**

In *trans*-polyacetylene $(\text{CH})_x$, each carbon atom is $sp^2$ hybridized. The three $sp^2$ hybrids form $\sigma$-bonds in the plane of the chain (two C–C and one C–H), localizing the $\sigma$-electrons in deep energy levels. The remaining unhybridized **$2p_z$ orbital** (perpendicular to the chain plane) contains one electron per carbon and is responsible for the electronic properties of interest.

Applying the tight-binding model to the chain of carbon $2p_z$ orbitals:
- The on-site energy $\alpha$ is the $2p_z$ atomic energy (reference).
- The transfer integral $\beta$ couples $2p_z$ orbitals on neighboring carbons.
- The resulting band has **width $W = 4 \vert \beta \vert$** and can hold $2N$ electrons (2 for spin).
- Since each carbon contributes one $2p_z$ electron, the chain has $N$ electrons filling the **lower half** of the band. The energy at the Fermi level is $\alpha$ (mid-band).

**Band gap from dimerization (4 marks):**

In the hypothetical **undimerized** chain (all C–C bonds equal), the $\pi$-band would be **half-filled** and the system would be a 1D metal with a **zero band gap**. However, the **Peierls instability** of 1D metals drives a **dimerization**: the C–C bonds alternate between a short "double-bond-like" length $a_1$ and a longer "single-bond-like" length $a_2$.

Define the **dimerization coordinate**:

$$u = \frac{a_1 - a_2}{2}$$

When $u \neq 0$, the unit cell doubles and the Brillouin zone halves. The two distinct hopping integrals depend on $u$ as:

$$\beta_1 = \beta_0 - \alpha_0 u \quad \text{(short bond)}, \quad \beta_2 = \beta_0 + \alpha_0 u \quad \text{(long bond)}$$

The **band gap** that opens at the new Brillouin zone edge is:

$$E_g = 2 \vert \beta_1 - \beta_2 \vert = 4 \alpha_0 \vert u \vert$$

Thus, the band gap is **linearly proportional** to the dimerization amplitude. This is the cornerstone result of the **Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model** for polyacetylene and explains why undoped polyacetylene is a semiconductor with $E_g \approx 1.5$ eV, despite having a half-filled $\pi$-band in the undimerized limit.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Tight-Binding Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/\sqrt{N}$ normalization factor** in the LCAO wavefunction. This is a **0.5–1 mark deduction** every time. Always include it explicitly.
>
> 2. **Confusing the on-site integral $\alpha$ with the transfer integral $\beta$.** Examiners specifically test: "What is $\alpha$? What is $\beta$?" Know the difference cold: $\alpha$ is **same site**, $\beta$ is **neighboring sites**.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in the cosine dispersion.** Students often write $E(k) = \alpha - 2\beta \cos(ka)$ instead of $+2\beta$. The sign of $\beta$ depends on whether you are using the bond-centered or site-centered convention. In the **standard convention**, $\beta < 0$ for a bonding ground state, and the band minimum occurs at $k = 0$.
>
> 4. **Omitting the discussion of the Peierls distortion** when the question concerns polyacetylene. This is a **favorite 2-mark question** in KTU exams. Always link the dimerization to the gap opening.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the orthogonal approximation explicitly** during the derivation. Examiners want to see you state the assumption $S_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$.
>
> 6. **Confusing bandwidth formulas:** In 1D, $W = 4 \vert \beta \vert$; in 3D simple cubic, $W = 12 \vert \beta \vert$. The general formula is $W = 2Z \vert \beta \vert$ where $Z$ is the coordination number.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tight-Binding Theory — Final Bullet-Point Summary:**
>
> - **Core idea:** Electrons in a crystal are built up from **atomic orbitals** at each lattice site, modulated by **Bloch phase factors**.
> - **Bloch wavefunction:** $\psi_k(\mathbf{r}) = (1/\sqrt{N}) \sum_j e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot \mathbf{R}_j} \phi_a(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{R}_j)$.
> - **Three integrals:** $\alpha$ (on-site), $\beta$ (transfer), $S$ (overlap).
> - **Dispersion (1D):** $E(k) = \alpha + 2\beta \cos(ka)$; bandwidth $W = 4 \vert \beta \vert$.
> - **Dispersion (simple cubic 3D):** $E(\mathbf{k}) = \alpha + 2\beta[\cos(k_x a) + \cos(k_y a) + \cos(k_z a)]$; bandwidth $W = 12 \vert \beta \vert$.
> - **General bandwidth formula:** $W = 2Z \vert \beta \vert$, where $Z$ is the coordination number.
> - **Effective mass:** $m^* = \hbar^2 / (\partial^2 E / \partial k^2)$.
> - **Group velocity:** $v_g = (1/\hbar)(\partial E / \partial k)$ — zero at Brillouin zone boundary (standing wave).
> - **Dimerized 1D chain (polyacetylene):** $E_{\pm}(k) = \pm \sqrt{\beta_1^2 + \beta_2^2 + 2\beta_1 \beta_2 \cos(ka)}$.
> - **Polyacetylene band gap:** $E_g = 2 \vert \beta_1 - \beta_2 \vert = 4 \alpha_0 \vert u \vert$ where $u$ is the dimerization coordinate.
> - **1D DOS:** $g(E) = 1 / (\pi a \sqrt{4\beta^2 - (E-\alpha)^2})$ — diverges at band edges (Van Hove singularities).
> - **Peierls distortion:** A 1D metal with a half-filled band is unstable to lattice dimerization, which opens a gap and lowers the total energy.
> - **Conducting polymer relevance:** The $\pi$ and $\pi^*$ bands of polyacetylene arise from tight-binding coupling of the carbon $2p_z$ orbitals; the system is a semiconductor due to Peierls-driven dimerization, not due to intrinsic atomic levels.
> - **Comparison:** Tight-binding is best for narrow bands, $d/f$ electrons, organic polymers; NFE is best for broad bands in simple metals.
> - **Mnemonic for the three integrals:** **"Alpha on site, Beta between, Same site overlap is One, Different site overlap is Small."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Band Structure of C–C Bonds** describes the quantum mechanical energy distribution of electronic states formed when carbon atoms in a polymer backbone interact through their valence orbitals. In the context of conducting polymers, this structure arises primarily from the overlap of **sp² hybridized** carbon atoms, where each carbon contributes one unhybridized **p_z orbital** oriented perpendicular to the polymer chain plane. The lateral overlap of these p_z orbitals creates a delocalized **π-electron system** that extends along the conjugated backbone, giving rise to discrete **molecular orbitals (MOs)** which, in an infinite polymeric chain, broaden into continuous **energy bands** — namely the **π (bonding) valence band** and the **π* (antibonding) conduction band**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEPOT863 – Module 1):**
> The band structure of C–C bonds is the foundational concept linking organic chemistry (hybridization, conjugation) to solid-state physics (energy bands, band gap, Fermi level). Every subsequent topic in conducting polymers — polyacetylene, polypyrrole, polyaniline, doping mechanisms — rests upon this framework.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a row of people standing in a line, each holding a bucket. When nobody is moving, the water inside each bucket sits still at its own level (isolated atomic orbitals). Now ask everyone to pass the bucket sideways to their neighbour. The water sloshes and forms waves that travel the entire length of the line — that is a **delocalized band**. The **higher-energy wave crests** represent the conduction band (π*) and the **lower-energy wave troughs** represent the valence band (π). The vertical distance between them is the **band gap (E_g)**. If the people are spaced too far apart (broken conjugation), the water cannot pass between buckets and the wave dies — that is an **insulator**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Planck's constant (h):** **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Boltzmann constant (k_B):** **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **C–C bond length (sp²):** **≈ 1.34 Å** (in polyenes); **1.42 Å** (in graphite/benzene)
- **Typical π–π* band gap of pristine trans-polyacetylene:** **≈ 1.4 – 1.5 eV**
- **Resonance integral (β) for C=C:** **≈ –2.5 to –3.0 eV**
- **Coulomb integral (α) for carbon 2p_z:** **≈ –11.4 eV** (relative to vacuum)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Band Gap (E_g):**
> The minimum energy required to promote an electron from the top of the valence band (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital, HOMO) to the bottom of the conduction band (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital, LUMO). For intrinsic conducting polymers, E_g typically lies between **0.5 eV and 3.5 eV**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hybridization of Carbon — The Origin of π Bands

Carbon's electronic configuration is **1s² 2s² 2p²**. To form the conjugated backbone of conducting polymers, one 2s electron is promoted to the empty 2p_z orbital, and the four valence orbitals mix to give:

- **sp³ hybridization:** Tetrahedral, 109.5° — present in saturated polymers (e.g., polyethylene, an **insulator** because no π system exists).
- **sp² hybridization:** Trigonal planar, 120° — present in polyacetylene, polythiophene, polypyrrole, polyaniline. Three σ-bonds lie in-plane; one **p_z orbital** stands perpendicular.
- **sp hybridization:** Linear, 180° — found in polyynes (less common in conducting polymer research).

> The **p_z orbital** is the sole contributor to the π-band structure in conducting polymers.

## 2.2 From Atomic Orbitals to Energy Bands — Stepwise Logic

1. **Step 1 — Atomic Orbital (AO) Stage:** Each isolated carbon 2p_z orbital has a single energy level, characterized by the Coulomb integral **α**.
2. **Step 2 — Dimer Stage (Ethylene Analogue):** When two p_z orbitals approach, Linear Combination of Atomic Orbitals (LCAO) produces two MOs: a lower-energy **bonding π** orbital and a higher-energy **antibonding π*** orbital, separated by **2|β|**.
3. **Step 3 — Oligomer Stage:** For N interacting p_z orbitals, N molecular orbitals form, with energies E_k = α + 2β cos(kπ/(N+1)) in the Hückel approximation. The MOs spread across the energy axis, forming an **MO ladder**.
4. **Step 4 — Polymeric Limit (N → ∞):** The discrete MO ladder becomes a quasi-continuous **energy band**. The top of the π band is the **HOMO** (Valence Band Edge, E_v) and the bottom of the π* band is the **LUMO** (Conduction Band Edge, E_c).
5. **Step 5 — Peierls Distortion (1D Instability):** A perfectly equispaced 1D chain (e.g., trans-polyacetylene) undergoes a **dimerization** — alternating short (≈ 1.36 Å) and long (≈ 1.44 Å) C–C bonds — opening a finite band gap. Without this, the 1D chain would be a metal.

## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' — Physical Reasoning

- **Why σ bands lie deeper than π bands:** σ overlap (head-on) is much stronger than π overlap (lateral), producing a larger resonance integral |β_σ| > |β_π|. The σ band therefore sits far below the π band and is essentially inert in conductivity discussions.
- **How conjugation length controls E_g:** As the number of conjugated repeat units N increases, the HOMO rises and the LUMO falls (because cos(kπ/(N+1)) spans a wider range as N→∞), **shrinking E_g** and red-shifting the optical absorption.
- **How doping modifies the band structure:** Chemical oxidation (p-doping) removes electrons from the π band, creating a **polaron** (radical cation with local lattice distortion), while reduction (n-doping) adds electrons to π*, creating a **polaron** (radical anion). At high doping, bipolarons merge into **soliton bands** within the original gap.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Coulomb integral | $\alpha = \langle \phi_i \vert \hat{H} \vert \phi_i \rangle$ | Energy of an electron in an isolated p_z AO | ≈ **−11.4 eV** |
| 2 | Resonance (bond) integral | $\beta = \langle \phi_i \vert \hat{H} \vert \phi_j \rangle$ | Strength of π-overlap between adjacent p_z AOs | **−2.5 to −3.0 eV** |
| 3 | Hückel MO energy of N-atom chain | $E_k = \alpha + 2\beta \cos\!\left(\dfrac{k\pi}{N+1}\right)$ | Discrete MO energies, k = 1, 2, …, N | eV |
| 4 | Ethylene π / π* energies | $E_{\pi} = \alpha + \beta$, $E_{\pi^{*}} = \alpha - \beta$ | Dimer limit (N = 2) | eV |
| 5 | Band gap of polyene (N large) | $E_g \approx \dfrac{4\vert\beta\vert}{N+1}$ | Decreases as 1/N with conjugation length | eV |
| 6 | Peierls gap (polyacetylene) | $E_g^{\text{Peierls}} = 4\delta$, $\delta \propto u$ | Opened by dimerization amplitude u | ≈ **1.4 eV** |
| 7 | Fermi level (intrinsic) | $E_F = \dfrac{E_c + E_v}{2}$ | Mid-gap for undoped chain | eV |
| 8 | Carrier density relation | $\sigma = n e \mu$ | Conductivity from doped band | S/cm |
| 9 | Band width | $W = 4\vert\beta\vert$ | Total π-band width in polyacetylene | ≈ **10–12 eV** |
| 10 | Optical absorption edge | $\lambda_{\text{onset}} = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$ | Cutoff wavelength from band gap | nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The sign convention for β is **negative** in chemistry (β ≈ −2.7 eV), which makes the bonding π orbital have energy **α + β** (lower than α) and the antibonding π* orbital have **α − β** (higher than α). Many KTU answer keys have historically flipped this sign; always show the algebra explicitly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the band structure of C–C bonds is **non-negotiable** in designing:
- **Organic Light-Emitting Diodes (OLEDs):** Choosing conjugated polymers with E_g matching the desired emission wavelength.
- **Organic Photovoltaics (OPVs):** Matching donor HOMO/LUMO with acceptor LUMO for charge separation.
- **Flexible Electronics & Sensors:** Tuning conductivity by controlling conjugation length and doping level.
- **Electrochromic Devices:** Band-gap engineering drives colour-switching upon polaron/bipolaron formation.
- **Biomedical Electrodes:** PEDOT:PSS coatings rely on doped polythiophene band structures for stable charge transfer.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hückel Molecular Orbital Energies for an N-Atom π Chain

We consider a linear chain of N carbon atoms, each contributing one p_z orbital $\phi_i$. The trial molecular orbital is the LCAO:

$$
\psi_k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{ki} \, \phi_i
$$

Applying the **Hückel approximations**:
- (i) Overlap integral $S_{ij} = \langle \phi_i \mid \phi_j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$
- (ii) $\langle \phi_i \mid \hat{H} \mid \phi_i \rangle = \alpha$
- (iii) $\langle \phi_i \mid \hat{H} \mid \phi_j \rangle = \beta$ if $i,j$ are adjacent, and **0** otherwise.

The secular determinant condition $\det(\mathbf{H} - E\mathbf{S}) = 0$ becomes:

$$
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha - E & \beta & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
\beta & \alpha - E & \beta & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & \beta & \alpha - E & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \beta \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \beta & \alpha - E
\end{pmatrix}_{N \times N} = 0
$$

Dividing each row by $\beta$ and substituting $x = \dfrac{\alpha - E}{\beta}$ reduces the determinant to a tridiagonal form whose eigenvalues are the roots of the N-th order Chebyshev polynomial of the second kind. The closed-form solution is:

$$
E_k = \alpha + 2\beta \cos\!\left( \frac{k\pi}{N+1} \right), \qquad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N
$$

Since β is negative for carbon (β ≈ −2.7 eV), the lowest energy is obtained at k = N (cos ≈ −1), giving $E_{\text{bottom}} = \alpha - 2|\beta|$, and the highest at k = 1 (cos ≈ +1), giving $E_{\text{top}} = \alpha + 2|\beta|$.

> **Textual interpretation of the derivation:** The secular determinant captures the quantum-mechanical coupling between neighbouring p_z orbitals; the tridiagonal structure encodes "nearest-neighbour" π-overlap, and its eigenvalues are quantized standing-wave solutions on a discrete 1-D lattice — the molecular orbital analogue of a particle in a box, but with periodic boundary conditions in the infinite-chain limit.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dimer (N = 2) Limit: Ethylene π / π*

For N = 2, the Hückel secular determinant is:

$$
\begin{vmatrix}
\alpha - E & \beta \\
\beta & \alpha - E
\end{vmatrix} = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
(\alpha - E)^2 - \beta^2 = 0
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\alpha - E = \pm \beta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad E = \alpha \mp \beta
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_{\pi} = \alpha + \beta \quad (\text{bonding, lower energy})
$$
$$
E_{\pi^*} = \alpha - \beta \quad (\text{antibonding, higher energy})
$$

**Energy splitting (band width of dimer):**

$$
\Delta E = E_{\pi^*} - E_{\pi} = (\alpha - \beta) - (\alpha + \beta) = -2\beta
$$

With β = −2.7 eV, this gives $\Delta E = 5.4$ eV, consistent with the experimental π–π* excitation of ethylene (≈ 5.4 eV, λ ≈ 230 nm).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Polyacetylene Dimerization (Peierls Gap)

Consider trans-polyacetylene with equal C–C bond lengths. The π-band in the infinite-chain limit is a half-filled metal. To model the **dimerization** (alternating short and long bonds), we assign two distinct resonance integrals:

$$
\beta_1 = \beta + \delta \beta \quad \text{(short bond)}, \qquad \beta_2 = \beta - \delta \beta \quad \text{(long bond)}
$$

The new Bloch solutions for the alternating chain yield band edges:

$$
E_{\text{top, bottom}} = \alpha \pm \sqrt{\beta_1^2 + \beta_2^2 + 2\beta_1\beta_2 \cos(ka)}
$$

The band gap at the zone boundary (k = π/a) is:

$$
E_g = 2 \sqrt{\beta_1^2 + \beta_2^2 - 2\beta_1\beta_2} = 2(\beta_1 - \beta_2) = 4\,\delta\beta
$$

Defining the dimerization amplitude $u = \dfrac{1}{2}(d_{\text{long}} - d_{\text{short}})$ and recognizing $\delta\beta \propto u$:

$$
E_g^{\text{Peierls}} = 4\,\delta\beta \propto u
$$

For trans-polyacetylene, the observed optical gap is **1.4 – 1.5 eV**, consistent with $u \approx 0.03$ Å.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Conjugation-Length Dependence of E_g

For an oligomer of N repeat units, the HOMO–LUMO gap from the Hückel formula is:

$$
E_g(N) = E_{k=1} - E_{k=N} = 2\beta\!\left[\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{N+1}\right) - \cos\!\left(\frac{N\pi}{N+1}\right)\right]
$$

Using the identity $\cos A - \cos B = -2 \sin\!\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$ and simplifying for large N:

$$
E_g(N) \approx 2\beta \cdot 2 \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\frac{N\pi/2}{N+1}\right) \cdot \frac{2}{N+1} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{approx}}
$$

A cleaner asymptotic expansion (Taylor series of cosine near π/2) yields:

$$
E_g(N) \approx \frac{4 \vert \beta \vert}{N+1} + \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{1}{N^3}\right)
$$

This inverse dependence on N explains why **longer conjugated chains have smaller band gaps** and absorb at longer wavelengths.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping of Concepts

| Concept | Atomic Orbital | Molecular Orbital | Polymeric Band | Engineering Signature |
|---------|---------------|-------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| σ framework | C sp² hybrids | σ-bonding MOs (deep) | σ band (far below E_F) | Skeletal integrity; no optical role |
| π bonding | C 2p_z | π-bonding MOs | π valence band (HOMO) | Sets ionization potential |
| π* antibonding | C 2p_z | π* antibonding MOs | π* conduction band (LUMO) | Sets electron affinity |
| Conjugation length N | — | MO ladder | Band width & gap | Optical absorption λ_onset |
| Dimerization (Peierls) | — | Bond-length alternation | Opens E_g | Optical colour of polyacetylene |
| p-Doping (oxidation) | — | Hole in π band | Polaron / bipolaron level | Electrical conductivity ↑↑ |
| n-Doping (reduction) | — | Electron in π* band | Polaron / bipolaron level | n-type conductivity |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEPOT863, Module 1]**
*Define the terms Coulomb integral (α) and Resonance integral (β) as used in Hückel Molecular Orbital (HMO) theory of conjugated systems. Why is β negative for carbon π-bonds?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Coulomb integral α** = $\langle \phi_i \mid \hat{H} \mid \phi_i \rangle$ represents the energy of an electron in an isolated p_z atomic orbital of carbon. Its value is ≈ **−11.4 eV** relative to vacuum. (1 mark)
- **Resonance integral β** = $\langle \phi_i \mid \hat{H} \mid \phi_j \rangle$ (i ≠ j, adjacent) measures the interaction energy between two neighbouring p_z orbitals. (1 mark)
- β is **negative** because the off-diagonal Hamiltonian matrix element of two overlapping atomic orbitals (with realistic potential) is negative, making the bonding combination α + β (lower than α) and the antibonding combination α − β (higher). (1 mark)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEPOT863, Module 1]**
*What is the Peierls distortion? How does it lead to a band gap in trans-polyacetylene?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Peierls distortion** is the spontaneous dimerization of a one-dimensional equispaced chain of atoms to lower its total energy, doubling the unit cell. (1 mark)
- In trans-polyacetylene, the originally metallic half-filled π-band becomes unstable; the C–C bonds alternate between short (≈ 1.36 Å) and long (≈ 1.44 Å) lengths, opening a **band gap E_g ≈ 1.4 eV** at the Brillouin-zone boundary k = π/a. (1 mark)
- This converts the 1-D "metal" into a semiconductor, with $E_g = 4\,\delta\beta \propto u$, where u is the dimerization amplitude. (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. An internal choice is offered between two full questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Derive the Hückel molecular orbital energies for a linear conjugated chain of N carbon atoms, each contributing one p_z orbital. Show that the band width equals $4|\beta|$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — LCAO ansatz.** The k-th molecular orbital is a linear combination of N atomic p_z orbitals:

$$
\psi_k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{ki}\, \phi_i \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hückel approximations.** $S_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$, $H_{ii} = \alpha$, $H_{ij} = \beta$ for adjacent atoms and 0 otherwise. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Secular determinant.** The condition for non-trivial solutions gives:

$$
\det(H_{ij} - E\,S_{ij}) = 0 \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

which leads to the tridiagonal determinant shown in Section 3.1. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Eigenvalue solution.** The closed-form Hückel energies are:

$$
E_k = \alpha + 2\beta \cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{N+1}\right), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N \quad \text{...(3)}
$$

(2 marks for stating and explaining this result.)

**Step 5 — Band width calculation.** The minimum (k = N) and maximum (k = 1) energies are:

$$
E_{\min} = \alpha - 2|\beta|, \qquad E_{\max} = \alpha + 2|\beta|
$$

Therefore the total π-band width is:

$$
W = E_{\max} - E_{\min} = 4|\beta| \quad \text{...(4)}
$$

(2 marks)

With β ≈ −2.7 eV, the band width is ≈ **10.8 eV**, consistent with photoemission measurements on trans-polyacetylene. (1 mark)

#### **(b)** Explain how the Hückel band gap of a polyene oligomer scales with the number of conjugated repeat units N. Estimate E_g for N = 10 and N = 50, taking β = −2.7 eV. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — HOMO–LUMO identification.** From Equation (3), the HOMO is the MO with k = N/2 (or nearest integer) and the LUMO is the next one. For simplicity, the HOMO–LUMO gap in the even-N case is:

$$
E_g(N) = E_{k = N/2 + 1} - E_{k = N/2} = 2\beta \!\left[\cos\!\left(\frac{(N/2+1)\pi}{N+1}\right) - \cos\!\left(\frac{N\pi/2}{N+1}\right)\right]
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Large-N asymptotic expansion.** Using the identity $\cos A - \cos B = -2 \sin\!\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$ and Taylor-expanding about π/2:

$$
E_g(N) \approx \frac{4|\beta|}{N+1} \quad \text{...(5)}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Numerical estimates.** With |β| = 2.7 eV:

- For **N = 10:** $E_g = 4 \times 2.7 / 11 = 0.982$ eV. (1 mark)
- For **N = 50:** $E_g = 4 \times 2.7 / 51 = 0.212$ eV. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.** The band gap shrinks as **1/N**, meaning longer conjugated chains absorb at longer wavelengths (red-shifted). For N = 50, the polymer behaves like a narrow-gap semiconductor; the optical absorption onset is:

$$
\lambda_{\text{onset}} = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1240 \text{ eV·nm}}{0.212 \text{ eV}} \approx 5849 \text{ nm}
$$

i.e., far into the infrared. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a)** With the help of a neat energy-level diagram, describe the formation of the π and π* bands in trans-polyacetylene. Discuss the role of Peierls distortion in opening a band gap. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Atomic origin.** Each sp²-hybridized C contributes one p_z orbital (energy α). In a hypothetical **equispaced** infinite chain, the N → ∞ limit of the Hückel energies gives a half-filled metallic band of width 4|β|. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Peierls instability.** A half-filled 1-D metal is unstable; the chain **dimerizes** into alternating short (1.36 Å) and long (1.44 Å) bonds, doubling the unit cell and halving the Brillouin zone. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Brillouin-zone folding.** Folding the band back onto the reduced zone opens a gap at the new zone boundary k = π/a, splitting the original band into a **lower π band (valence)** and an **upper π* band (conduction)**. (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Energy-level diagram (textual description).** A vertical energy axis shows:
- Lower: σ band (deep, from sp² hybrids).
- Middle-low: π valence band, fully filled.
- Middle: gap E_g ≈ 1.4 eV.
- Middle-high: π* conduction band, empty.
- Upper: σ* band (very high).

(2 marks)

**Step 5 — Numerical result.** $E_g = 4\delta\beta \approx 1.4$ eV for trans-polyacetylene, in agreement with optical absorption onset near 885 nm. (1 mark)

#### **(b)** Discuss how chemical doping (p-type and n-type) modifies the π-band structure of a conducting polymer. Sketch the polaron and bipolaron levels inside the band gap. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pristine (undoped) state.** The π valence band is full and the π* conduction band is empty; the Fermi level lies near mid-gap; the conductivity is low (≈ 10⁻¹⁰ S/cm). (1 mark)

**Step 2 — p-type doping (oxidation).** A strong electron acceptor (e.g., I₂, FeCl₃) removes an electron from the π band. The missing electron is a **hole**, but the polymer chain locally distorts around the charge, creating a **polaron** — a radical cation coupled to a geometric relaxation of ≈ 4–6 C–C bond lengths. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Bipolaron formation.** A second oxidation step near the same site removes the unpaired electron, leaving a **bipolaron** (dication with no spin) and a deeper lattice distortion. The bipolaron introduces **two new energy levels** in the original gap — one bonding-like (occupied) and one antibonding-like (empty), separated by a small Hubbard U. (2 marks)

**Step 4 — n-type doping (reduction).** An electron donor (e.g., Na, K) injects an electron into the π* band, creating a **negative polaron** (radical anion) and, on further doping, a **negative bipolaron**. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — High-doping regime.** At dopant concentrations exceeding ≈ 1 mol%, the polaron/bipolaron levels broaden into **impurity bands** that may eventually overlap with the π or π* edges, yielding metallic-like conductivities of 10² – 10³ S/cm (and up to 10⁵ S/cm in highly oriented polyacetylene). (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Diagram (textual).** Energy-axis sketch:
- π band (top, partially emptied by p-doping).
- Polaron level (singly occupied, just above π band edge for p-doping).
- Bipolaron levels (two, one filled and one empty, deeper in gap).
- π* band (bottom, partially filled by n-doping).
- E_F shifts towards the polaron/bipolaron level upon doping. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PEPOT863 Band-Structure Questions:**
> 1. **Sign of β** — Forgetting that β is negative for carbon π-bonds, leading to an inverted band diagram. KTU examiners explicitly deduct **½ to 1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing σ and π bands** — Many students write that σ bands contribute to conduction in polyacetylene. They do not. Always emphasize that **only π and π\* bands** participate in electronic conduction.
> 3. **Missing Peierls argument** — A 14-mark question on polyacetylene band gap that omits the Peierls distortion typically loses **2–3 marks** because the answer appears "metallic-like" without justification for the gap.
> 4. **Skipping the Hückel approximations** — Stating $E_k = \alpha + 2\beta \cos(k\pi/(N+1))$ without listing the three Hückel approximations (zero overlap, α on diagonal, β only between neighbours) loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Doping vs. band gap** — Examiners often ask whether doping changes E_g. The technically correct answer: doping introduces **in-gap states (polarons/bipolarons)**, not a new E_g. Mis-stating this as "doping reduces E_g" is a **2-mark deduction** in valuation.
> 6. **Unit errors** — Band gaps must be in **eV**, wavelengths in **nm** or **μm**, conductivities in **S/cm**. Mixing units is a frequent minor penalty.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Band Structure of C–C Bonds**

- ☐ Carbon in conducting polymers is **sp² hybridized**; only the **p_z** orbital builds the π-band.
- ☐ **Coulomb integral α** ≈ −11.4 eV; **resonance integral β** ≈ −2.7 eV for C 2p_z.
- ☐ For a chain of N p_z orbitals: $E_k = \alpha + 2\beta \cos(k\pi/(N+1))$, k = 1 … N.
- ☐ **Band width** of the π-band in the infinite chain = **4|β|** ≈ 10–12 eV.
- ☐ **Peierls distortion** opens a finite band gap in 1-D conjugated chains; without it, trans-polyacetylene would be a metal.
- ☐ **HOMO–LUMO gap** in oligomers scales as $E_g \approx 4|\beta|/(N+1)$ → **smaller for longer conjugation**.
- ☐ **σ bands** lie far below the π band and are electronically inert in conducting polymers.
- ☐ **Doping** (p- or n-type) introduces **polaron / bipolaron states inside the gap**, dramatically increasing conductivity.
- ☐ Typical pristine conductivities: **10⁻¹⁰ S/cm**; doped: **10² – 10⁵ S/cm** (polyacetylene record).
- ☐ Master the **Hückel secular determinant** derivation — it is the single most repeated derivation in PEPOT863.
- ☐ Remember: **band gap in eV ↔ wavelength in nm** via $\lambda(\text{nm}) = 1240/E_g(\text{eV})$.
- ☐ Always specify **which approximation** (Hückel, extended Hückel, PPP, DFT) you are using when writing E_g.
- ☐ Polaron = radical ± charge + local lattice distortion; **bipolaron** = doubly charged, no spin.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pendant Groups and Electronic States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT863 - CONDUCTING POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pendant Groups and Electronic States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Pendant Groups?

In polymer chemistry, **pendant groups** (also called side groups, side chains, or substituents) are the chemical moieties that are covalently attached to the **main polymer backbone** but are not part of the primary chain itself. They hang off the backbone like ornaments on a chain, and although they do not form the structural skeleton, they profoundly influence the chemical, physical, electronic, and processing behavior of the polymer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Pendant groups are functional substituents attached laterally to the conjugated polymer backbone. They modify the polymer's electronic structure by altering electron density, backbone planarity, conjugation length, inter-chain packing, and consequently the band gap and charge transport characteristics.*

In conducting polymers such as polythiophene, polypyrrole, polyaniline, and poly(*p*-phenylene vinylene), the **π-conjugated backbone** is the seat of electronic conduction. Pendant groups can be:
- **Alkyl / Aryl chains** (e.g., –C₆H₁₃, –C₁₂H₂₅) → primarily improve solubility and processability.
- **Electron-donating groups (EDG)** (e.g., –OCH₃, –OH, –NH₂, –SCH₃) → raise the HOMO level.
- **Electron-withdrawing groups (EWG)** (e.g., –NO₂, –CN, –COOH, –F, –Cl) → lower the LUMO level.
- **Bulky / steric groups** (e.g., –phenyl, –tert-butyl) → induce backbone twisting and break conjugation.
- **Chiral / liquid crystalline groups** → introduce secondary functional properties.

### 1.2 What are Electronic States?

Electronic states refer to the **discrete energy levels** that electrons of a molecule (or polymer chain) can occupy, governed by quantum mechanics and molecular orbital theory. In conducting polymers, the most important electronic states are:

| Electronic State | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Neutral / undoped state | $\text{P}^0$ | Pristine polymer, no charges on chain |
| Polaron (radical cation) | $\text{P}^{+\bullet}$ | Single positive charge + unpaired electron + lattice distortion |
| Polaron (radical anion) | $\text{P}^{-\bullet}$ | Single negative charge + unpaired electron |
| Bipolaron (dication) | $\text{P}^{2+}$ | Two positive charges, no unpaired electrons |
| Bipolaron (dianion) | $\text{P}^{2-}$ | Two negative charges, no unpaired electrons |
| Soliton | $\text{S}$ / $\overline{\text{S}}$ | Domain-wall defect in degenerate-ground-state polymers (e.g., *trans*-polyacetylene) |
| Polaron-exciton | $\text{P}^{*}$ | Bound electron-hole pair, optically excited |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Decorated Ladder:**
> Imagine a **π-conjugated polymer backbone** as a long ladder. Each **pendant group** is a decoration (a flag, a light, a magnet) tied to one of the ladder's rungs. Some flags are **electron-donating** (they "push" electrons into the ladder), while others are **electron-withdrawing** (they "pull" electrons out). **Bulky flags** make the ladder twist and break its linearity — just as steric pendant groups break conjugation. **Electronic states** (polaron, bipolaron) are analogous to **ripples traveling down the ladder** when the decorations interact with the ladder's structural vibrations — they are *charge carriers bound to local geometric distortions*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \, m/s}$ |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, J \cdot s}$ |
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, J/K}$ |
| Electronic charge | $e$ | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, C}$ |
| Band gap of pristine polythiophene | $E_g$ | $\mathbf{\approx 2.0 \, eV}$ |
| Band gap of PEDOT | $E_g$ | $\mathbf{\approx 1.6 \, eV}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Pendant Group Effects

#### (A) Electronic Effects

**(i) Inductive Effect (σ-bond mediated):**
- Through-bond polarization of σ-electrons.
- **–I effect** (electron-withdrawing): –F, –Cl, –CN, –COOH
- **+I effect** (electron-donating): –CH₃, –C₂H₅, alkyl groups

**(ii) Resonance / Mesomeric Effect (π-system mediated):**
- Delocalization through the π-system.
- **–M / –R effect** (withdrawing): –NO₂, –CN, –C=O
- **+M / +R effect** (donating): –NH₂, –OH, –OCH₃, –N(CH₃)₂

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pendant groups that **donate** π-electrons raise the **HOMO** energy, while those that **withdraw** lower the **LUMO** energy. In both cases, the **band gap is reduced**, which is highly desirable for intrinsic conducting polymers.

#### (B) Steric Effects

- Bulky pendant groups introduce **torsional strain** between adjacent repeat units.
- The dihedral angle ($\theta$) between rings deviates from the ideal $0°$ (planar) or $180°$ (anti-planar).
- This **breaks π-orbital overlap** and shortens the **effective conjugation length** ($N_{\text{eff}}$).

#### (C) Secondary Effects

- **Solubility enhancement** → alkyl/alkoxy side chains.
- **Self-assembly** → regioregular P3HT forms π-stacked lamellae.
- **Inter-chain packing** → affects 3D charge transport.

### 2.2 Electronic States — Energy Picture

In an undoped conducting polymer:

$$\text{HOMO} \; \rightarrow \; \text{LUMO}, \quad E_g = E_{\text{LUMO}} - E_{\text{HOMO}}$$

Upon doping (chemical or electrochemical), charge is injected and **new states emerge within the band gap**:

1. **Polaron level** → singly-occupied, lies near mid-gap.
2. **Bipolaron levels** → two empty (or filled) levels, symmetric about the gap center.
3. **Soliton level** → mid-gap state, characteristic of *trans*-polyacetylene (degenerate ground state).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_g = h c \, / \, \lambda_{\text{onset}}$ | Optical band gap from UV-Vis onset | $\lambda$ in nm, $E_g$ in eV |
| 2 | $E_g = E_{\text{LUMO}} - E_{\text{HOMO}}$ | Electrochemical band gap | eV |
| 3 | $E_{\text{HOMO}} = - \left( E_{\text{ox,onset}} + 4.8 \right)$ | HOMO from CV oxidation onset | eV (vs. vacuum) |
| 4 | $E_{\text{LUMO}} = - \left( E_{\text{red,onset}} + 4.8 \right)$ | LUMO from CV reduction onset | eV |
| 5 | $E_g^{\text{opt}} = E_g^{\text{elec}} - E_{\text{bind}}$ | Optical vs. electrochemical gap | $E_{\text{bind}}$ = exciton binding energy |
| 6 | $\beta \cos\theta$ | Resonance integral modulated by dihedral angle | $\theta$ = torsional angle |
| 7 | $N_{\text{eff}} \propto 1 / \left(1 + \cos^2\theta \right)$ | Effective conjugation length vs. planarity | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\chi_{\text{Hammett}}(\sigma_p)$ | Hammett substituent constant | quantifies electronic effect |
| 9 | $\Delta E_g \approx -k \cdot \sigma_p$ | Linear free-energy correlation | $k$ = reaction constant |
| 10 | $\rho_\pi = e \cdot n_{\text{carrier}}$ | Charge density along chain | C/m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - In **organic solar cells (OSCs)** → pendant groups tune the band gap to match the solar spectrum.
> - In **OLEDs** → HOMO/LUMO alignment controls charge injection.
> - In **organic field-effect transistors (OFETs)** → regioregular alkyl chains maximize field-effect mobility.
> - In **biosensors** → functional pendant groups (e.g., –COOH, –NH₂) provide anchor points for biomolecules.

### 2.4 Substituent Effect Quantification — Hammett Equation

The **Hammett linear free-energy relationship** quantifies electronic effects of *meta* and *para* substituents on aromatic systems:

$$\log \left( \frac{K}{K_0} \right) = \rho \cdot \sigma_p$$

where:
- $K, K_0$ = reaction equilibrium constants (substituted vs. unsubstituted),
- $\rho$ = reaction constant (depends on the reaction type, not the substituent),
- $\sigma_p$ = **Hammett substituent constant** for the *para* position.

For conducting polymers, an analogous **band-gap correlation** is often used:

$$\Delta E_g \;\approx\; -\, \rho_{\text{polymer}} \cdot \sigma_p$$

Electron-donating groups ($\sigma_p < 0$) → $E_g$ decreases.
Electron-withdrawing groups ($\sigma_p > 0$) → $E_g$ also tends to decrease (via LUMO stabilization).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Both EDG and EWG can lower the band gap**, but through *different mechanisms*: EDG raises HOMO; EWG lowers LUMO. This is a frequently-tested KTU concept!
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Band Gap of a Polyene Chain from Hückel Theory

**Goal:** Derive the energy gap $E_g$ for a linear conjugated polyene with $N$ repeat units.

**Step 1 — Set up the secular determinant.**
For a linear polyene with $N$ carbon atoms in a π-conjugated system, the Hückel secular determinant is:

$$
\begin{vmatrix}
\alpha - E & \beta & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
\beta & \alpha - E & \beta & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & \beta & \alpha - E & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \beta \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \beta & \alpha - E
\end{vmatrix}_{N \times N} = 0
$$

where $\alpha$ = Coulomb integral, $\beta$ = resonance integral (negative quantity, $\beta \approx -2.5$ eV for C=C).

**Step 2 — Diagonalize the matrix.**
The roots of the secular equation for a linear chain are well known (analogy to particle-in-a-box):

$$
E_k = \alpha + 2 \beta \cos \left( \frac{k \pi}{N+1} \right), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N
$$

**Step 3 — Identify HOMO and LUMO.**
- For $N$ even, there are $N/2$ filled and $N/2$ empty levels.
- HOMO corresponds to $k = N/2$:

$$
E_{\text{HOMO}} = \alpha + 2 \beta \cos \left( \frac{(N/2) \pi}{N+1} \right)
$$

- LUMO corresponds to $k = N/2 + 1$:

$$
E_{\text{LUMO}} = \alpha + 2 \beta \cos \left( \frac{(N/2+1) \pi}{N+1} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the band gap.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_g &= E_{\text{LUMO}} - E_{\text{HOMO}} \\
&= 2\beta \left[ \cos \left( \frac{(N/2+1)\pi}{N+1} \right) - \cos \left( \frac{(N/2)\pi}{N+1} \right) \right] \\
&\xrightarrow{N \to \infty} 2\beta \left[ \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) - \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \right] \\
&\Rightarrow E_g \xrightarrow{N \to \infty} 0 \;\; \text{(for a Peierls-distorted system, becomes } \sim 1.4 \text{ eV)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, **Peierls distortion** doubles the unit cell of *trans*-polyacetylene, opening a finite band gap of about **1.4–1.8 eV**. The naive Hückel result $E_g \to 0$ is the metallic limit — a real polymer is unstable against bond-length alternation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Torsional Twist on Band Gap

**Step 1 — Resonance integral under twist.**
When adjacent rings are twisted by dihedral angle $\theta$ (relative to planar $\theta = 0$), the π-overlap integral is modulated:

$$
\beta(\theta) = \beta_0 \cos\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Modified band gap.**
Substituting $\beta(\theta)$ in the Hückel gap expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_g(\theta) &= E_g^{(0)} \cdot \cos\theta \\
\text{where } E_g^{(0)} &\text{ is the planar band gap.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation.**

$$
\boxed{\;\theta \uparrow \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \beta \downarrow \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E_g \uparrow\;}
$$

This is precisely why **bulky pendant groups increase the band gap** by twisting the backbone out of planarity.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optical Band Gap from UV-Vis Onset

The **onset wavelength** $\lambda_{\text{onset}}$ of the π→π* absorption band in a UV-Vis spectrum gives the **optical band gap** via Planck's relation:

$$
E_g^{\text{opt}} = \frac{h c}{\lambda_{\text{onset}}}
$$

**Step-by-step numerical example:**

Given: $\lambda_{\text{onset}} = 620 \text{ nm}$ (for a typical polythiophene derivative).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_g^{\text{opt}} &= \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J·s}) \times (3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s})}{620 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}} \\
&= \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \, \text{J·m}}{6.20 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}} \\
&= 3.206 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J} \\
&= \frac{3.206 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \, \text{eV} \\
&\approx 2.00 \, \text{eV}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always convert Joules to electron-volts using $1 \, \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J}$. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks if units are inconsistent.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pendant Group vs. Property

| Pendant Group Type | Example | Effect on $E_g$ | Effect on Conductivity | Effect on Solubility | Effect on Planarity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alkyl (short) | –CH₃, –C₂H₅ | Slight increase (steric) | Marginal | High ↑↑ | Distorts |
| Alkyl (long, regioregular) | –C₆H₁₃ (P3HT) | Optimal | High ↑ | High ↑↑ | Maintains |
| Alkoxy | –OCH₃, –OC₁₂H₂₅ | Decrease ↓ | High ↑ | High ↑↑ | Maintains (PEDOT) |
| Electron-donating (–NH₂) | Polyaniline | Decrease (HOMO ↑) | Moderate ↑ | Moderate | Maintains |
| Electron-withdrawing (–CN) | CN-PPV | Decrease (LUMO ↓) | Moderate ↑ | Low | Maintains |
| Bulky (–phenyl) | PTPTP | Increase (steric) ↓ | Low | High | Distorts ↑↑ |
| Fluorinated (–F) | F-PT | Decrease (LUMO ↓) | High ↑ | Low | Maintains |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Pendant Group Action on a Conducting Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Polymer Backbone pi conjugated chain] --> nodeB[Pendant Group attached laterally]
    nodeB --> nodeC1[Electronic Effect]
    nodeB --> nodeC2[Steric Effect]
    nodeB --> nodeC3[Secondary Effect]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1[HOMO level shift]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD2[LUMO level shift]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE1[Torsional twist angle theta]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE2[Reduced pi overlap]
    nodeC3 --> nodeF1[Solubility change]
    nodeC3 --> nodeF2[Inter chain packing]
    nodeD1 --> nodeG[Electronic States modified]
    nodeD2 --> nodeG
    nodeE2 --> nodeG
    nodeF1 --> nodeH[Band gap Eg and carrier mobility mu]
    nodeF2 --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Final Conductivity Sigma]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Electronic States in Conducting Polymers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateA[Neutral State P zero] -->|oxidation| stateB[Polaron P plus radical]
    stateB -->|further oxidation| stateC[Bipolaron P 2 plus]
    stateA -->|photoexcitation| stateD[Exciton bound e h]
    stateD -->|dissociation| stateB
    stateA -->|doping in trans PA| stateE[Soliton S]
    stateE -->|pairing| stateF[Neutral Soliton pair]
    stateB -->|pairing| stateC
    style stateA fill:#E8F4F8
    style stateB fill:#FFF4E6
    style stateC fill:#FFE6E6
    style stateD fill:#F0E6FF
    style stateE fill:#E6FFE6
    style stateF fill:#FFFFCC
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Polaron & Bipolaron Formation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Neutral conjugated chain]) --> step1[Remove one electron via oxidation]
    step1 --> step2[Single positive charge created on chain]
    step2 --> step3[Lattice distorts locally around the charge]
    step3 --> polaron[Polaron: radical cation with localized geometric distortion]
    polaron --> step4[Remove second electron from same site]
    step4 --> step5[Two positive charges, no unpaired electron]
    step5 --> bipolaron[Bipolaron: dication with stronger lattice distortion]
    polaron -.->|optical transition| polaronExcited[Photoexcited polaron level]
    bipolaron -.->|optical transition| bipolaronExcited[Photoexcited bipolaron level]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** In the second flowchart, the colour coding corresponds to:
> - Light blue → Neutral ground state
> - Light orange → Polaron
> - Light red → Bipolaron
> - Light purple → Excitonic states
> - Light green → Solitonic states
>
> These diagrams map directly to the **energy-level structure** you will draw in your KTU answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define pendant groups in conducting polymers. With one example, explain how an electron-donating pendant group affects the band gap of a conducting polymer.*

**Model Answer:**
Pendant groups are chemical substituents covalently attached to the main conjugated backbone of a polymer, protruding laterally from the chain. In conducting polymers, they are crucial because they influence electronic properties (band gap, charge transport), solubility, and processability.

**Example:** Poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) — here, the –C₆H₁₃ hexyl group is the pendant group attached at the 3-position of thiophene.

**Effect of an EDG on band gap:**
An electron-donating group (e.g., –OCH₃ in PEDOT) raises the energy of the **HOMO** by donating π-electron density into the conjugated system. If the HOMO is raised while the LUMO is comparatively unaffected, the gap

$$
E_g = E_{\text{LUMO}} - E_{\text{HOMO}}
$$

decreases. Hence, **EDGs reduce the band gap** and shift the absorption to longer wavelengths (red-shift).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between a polaron and a bipolaron in conducting polymers.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Polaron | Bipolaron |
|---|---|---|
| Charge | Single (+ or −) | Double (2+ or 2−) |
| Spin | Unpaired electron ($S = 1/2$) — **radical** | No unpaired electron ($S = 0$) — **spinless** |
| Lattice distortion | Local, moderate | Local, stronger (more extended) |
| Energy levels | Two levels in the gap (one singly occupied) | Two empty/filled levels in the gap |
| Formation | First oxidation / reduction step | Second oxidation / reduction of same site |
| Stability | Less stable | More thermodynamically stable (above a threshold doping) |

---

### Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With suitable energy-level diagrams, explain the formation of polarons and bipolarons in poly(p-phenylene vinylene) (PPV). Discuss how the introduction of an electron-withdrawing –CN pendant group alters the electronic states and band gap. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the optical band gap of a conducting polymer using Hückel molecular orbital theory for a 6-ring oligomer of polythiophene. Comment on the effect of dihedral twist on the effective conjugation length. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **PPV neutral state:** The π-system is fully delocalized along the backbone; HOMO is fully occupied, LUMO empty; band gap $E_g \approx 2.5$ eV.
2. **Polaron formation:** Upon removal of one electron (oxidation), a radical cation appears; the local geometry distorts to a quinoid-like form around the charge. Two new energy levels appear in the band gap — one singly-occupied (polaron level) and one empty (higher-lying polaron* level).
3. **Bipolaron formation:** Upon removal of a second electron, the unpaired electron is also removed, leaving a spinless dication. The lattice distortion is more pronounced, and the two bipolaron levels lie symmetrically about the gap centre.
4. **Effect of –CN group (EWG):**
   - The –CN group, through its **–M (resonance-withdrawing)** and **–I (inductive-withdrawing)** effects, **lowers the LUMO** of PPV.
   - New derivative: **CN-PPV** (poly(2,5-dialkoxy-1,4-phenylene vinylene) co-polymerised with –CN units).
   - Lowered LUMO + relatively unchanged HOMO → **reduced band gap** (≈ 1.6–1.8 eV).
   - Electron affinity is enhanced → better electron injection in OLEDs.
   - Steric effect of –CN is small, so planarity and conjugation are preserved.

**Model Solution (b):**

From the Hückel derivation (see Section 3.1), for $N = 6$:

$$
E_k = \alpha + 2\beta \cos\left(\frac{k\pi}{7}\right), \quad k = 1,2,3,4,5,6
$$

Filling $N = 6$ electrons (one per carbon), the HOMO is $k = 3$ and LUMO is $k = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{HOMO}} &= \alpha + 2\beta \cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{7}\right) = \alpha + 2\beta (0.2225) \\
E_{\text{LUMO}} &= \alpha + 2\beta \cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{7}\right) = \alpha + 2\beta (-0.6235) \\
E_g &= E_{\text{LUMO}} - E_{\text{HOMO}} = 2\beta(-0.6235 - 0.2225) = 2\beta(-0.846)
\end{aligned}
$$

With $\beta \approx -2.5$ eV:

$$
E_g = 2 \times (-2.5) \times (-0.846) \approx 4.23 \, \text{eV} \quad \text{(oligomer limit)}
$$

As $N \to \infty$, $E_g$ decreases and saturates at the polymer limit (~2.0 eV for polythiophene).

**Dihedral twist effect:**

$$
E_g(\theta) = E_g^{(0)} \cos\theta
$$

For bulky pendant groups, $\theta \to 30°$–$60°$, causing:

$$
E_g(\theta) > E_g^{(0)} \quad \text{and} \quad N_{\text{eff}} \ll N_{\text{total}}
$$

Planarity is therefore critical to maximize conjugation.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of solitons in *trans*-polyacetylene. Why does this electronic state NOT appear in non-degenerate ground-state polymers like polythiophene? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two polythiophene derivatives are studied: P3HT (regioregular, hexyl substituent) and PTPTP (phenyl-substituted). P3HT exhibits conductivity $\sigma_1$ and band gap $E_{g,1}$; PTPTP exhibits $\sigma_2$ and $E_{g,2}$. Using substituent effect arguments, justify which polymer has the lower band gap and which has higher solubility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

*Trans*-polyacetylene has a **degenerate ground state**: the two resonance structures (single–double–single–double vs. double–single–double–single bond alternation) are **isoenergetic**. This degeneracy permits a topological defect — the **soliton** — which is a domain wall separating two equivalent ground-state phases.

- A neutral soliton (S) is a radical with an unpaired electron at the domain wall, no net charge.
- A positively charged soliton (S⁺) is spinless (no unpaired electron), carrying +$e$.
- A negatively charged soliton (S⁻) is similarly spinless.

These states appear at **mid-gap** in the density of states.

**Why solitons don't appear in polythiophene:**
Polythiophene's aromatic form is energetically much more stable than its quinoid form (a non-degenerate ground state). The energy cost of creating a domain wall is finite, so the system prefers to form **polarons and bipolarons** rather than solitons. In non-degenerate polymers, polarons and bipolarons are the stable charge-carrying species.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Band gap comparison:**
- P3HT has a hexyl group at the 3-position. The hexyl chain is weakly electron-donating (+I effect) but primarily introduces a small steric twist (~20°). It does not strongly affect HOMO/LUMO levels.
- PTPTP has bulky phenyl groups, which introduce **strong steric hindrance**, twisting the backbone significantly. This breaks π-conjugation, reducing $N_{\text{eff}}$ and **increasing $E_{g,2}$**.

$$
\therefore \quad E_{g,1} < E_{g,2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{P3HT has the LOWER band gap.}
$$

**Solubility comparison:**
- P3HT's long hexyl chains impart excellent solubility in common organic solvents (chloroform, toluene).
- PTPTP's rigid, planar phenyl groups promote π-stacking and aggregation, reducing solubility.

$$
\therefore \quad S_1 > S_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{P3HT is MORE soluble.}
$$

**Conductivity consequence:**
P3HT exhibits **higher conductivity** due to (i) better solubility enabling film formation, (ii) regioregular packing into lamellar stacks for 2D charge transport, and (iii) lower band gap allowing easier carrier generation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. ❌ Confusing **–I (inductive)** and **–M (mesomeric)** effects — write explicitly which mechanism dominates for your chosen group.
> 2. ❌ Forgetting that **both EDG and EWG can reduce the band gap** but via *different* mechanisms (HOMO ↑ vs. LUMO ↓).
> 3. ❌ In soliton questions, students often forget to mention that *trans*-polyacetylene has a **degenerate ground state** — this is the *root cause* of soliton formation.
> 4. ❌ In numerical band-gap calculations, leaving the answer in Joules instead of converting to eV (lose 1 mark).
> 5. ❌ Confusing **polaron** (radical) with **bipolaron** (spinless dication).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Pendant groups** = substituents attached laterally to the conjugated backbone.
- ✅ Pendant groups affect polymers via **electronic effects (inductive, mesomeric)**, **steric effects**, and **secondary effects (solubility, packing)**.
- ✅ **Electron-donating groups** raise HOMO; **electron-withdrawing groups** lower LUMO — **both can reduce the band gap**.
- ✅ **Bulky pendant groups** → dihedral twist $\theta$ ↑ → $\beta \cos\theta$ ↓ → $E_g$ ↑.
- ✅ **Electronic states** in conducting polymers: neutral → polaron → bipolaron (upon progressive doping).
- ✅ **Solitons** appear ONLY in degenerate ground-state polymers (e.g., *trans*-polyacetylene).
- ✅ **Polarons** carry both charge and spin ($S = 1/2$); **bipolarons** carry charge only ($S = 0$).
- ✅ Optical band gap: $E_g^{\text{opt}} = h c \, / \, \lambda_{\text{onset}}$ — convert to **eV**.
- ✅ Electrochemical band gap: $E_g = E_{\text{LUMO}} - E_{\text{HOMO}}$ from cyclic voltammetry.
- ✅ Effective conjugation length $N_{\text{eff}}$ is reduced by torsion; $E_g \propto \cos\theta$.
- ✅ **Hammett equation** $\log(K/K_0) = \rho \sigma_p$ quantifies substituent electronic effect.
- ✅ Examples to remember: **P3HT, PEDOT, PPV, CN-PPV, polyaniline, polypyrrole** — note their key pendant groups and applications.
- ✅ Engineering relevance: **OSCs, OLEDs, OFETs, sensors, anticorrosion coatings, EMI shielding**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron-Hole Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT863 - CONDUCTING POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electron-Hole Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Electron-Hole Interaction in Conducting Polymers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> In a conjugated conducting polymer, an **electron-hole interaction** refers to the coupled response of the π-electron system and the polymer backbone lattice to an added or removed charge. When an electron is injected (doping/photoexcitation), it does not remain a free Bloch electron; instead, it self-localises by dragging a local bond-length distortion with it. This composite quasi-particle — charge + lattice deformation + spin — is called a **polaron**. Two like charges sharing a single distortion form a **bipolaron**, while in *trans*-polyacetylene a neutral or charged domain wall — a topological defect — is called a **soliton**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Baton-Passing Race"

Imagine a long line of runners on a track where each runner must hold the baton in their right hand to keep the rhythm (the alternating C–C single/double bonds). Now:

- A **hole** = a missing baton in one runner's hand. The neighbouring runner must stretch across to pass the baton, *locally slowing the cadence* (lattice distortion). The empty-handed spot is the **polaron**.
- An **electron** (extra baton) = a runner now has two batons. Again, the rhythm in that region is disturbed — a **negative polaron** forms.
- If **two extra charges** sit on adjacent runners, they share one big rhythmic disturbance — a **bipolaron**.
- In *trans*-polyacetylene, the two phases of the rhythm (left-foot-leading vs right-foot-leading) can meet at a single point without friction — this neutral meeting point is a **neutral soliton**, a topological kink.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The interaction is *not* a Coulomb force alone. The **electron-phonon coupling** (lattice distortion energy) is the dominant contribution. This is what makes conducting polymers fundamentally different from inorganic semiconductors, where the lattice barely responds to a charge carrier.

### 1.2 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon–carbon bond length (doped PA) | $a$ | **1.22–1.44** | Å |
| Phonon (C–C stretch) frequency | $\Omega_0$ | **0.12 eV/ℏ** | eV |
| Dimerisation gap (Peierls) | $2\Delta_0$ | **0.7–1.4** | eV |
| Electron-phonon coupling | $\alpha$ | **4.1** | eV/Å |
| Spinless degeneracy of ground state | — | **2** (for *trans*-PA) | — |
| Optical absorption (polaron) | $E_{P}$ | **~0.5 eV below gap** | eV |

> [!TIP]
> Always quote energies in **eV** when comparing to the Peierls gap. Distances in **Å** match the C–C bond scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does the Lattice Distort? — The Peierls Instability

A one-dimensional metal (the hypothetical undistorted polyacetylene) is *unstable* against a doubling of the unit cell — every other C–C bond shortens. This is the **Peierls Instability**.

**Step-wise logic:**
- The π-band is half-filled in an equidistant chain.
- A periodic lattice distortion of wavevector $2k_F$ opens a gap $2\Delta_0$ at the Fermi level.
- Electronic energy is lowered by an amount $\sim \Delta_0^2 / \pi t$ per site.
- Elastic cost of distortion is $\sim \tfrac{1}{2} K u_0^2$ per site.
- Net energy gain → spontaneous dimerisation.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the foundation on which **all** of conducting-polymer charge-carrier physics is built. Without the Peierls gap, there would be no need for solitons, polarons or bipolarons.

## 2.2 The Su–Schrieffer–Heeger (SSH) Hamiltonian

The workhorse model for electron-hole interaction in conducting polymers is the **SSH Hamiltonian** (1979, Su, Schrieffer, Heeger). It contains three terms:

$$
H_{SSH} \;=\; -\!\sum_{n,\sigma}\!\bigl(t_n\, c^{\dagger}_{n+1,\sigma}\, c_{n,\sigma} + \text{h.c.}\bigr) \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\,K\!\sum_{n}\!(u_{n+1}-u_n)^2 \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\,M\!\sum_{n}\!\dot{u}_n^{\,2}
$$

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $-t_n c^{\dagger}_{n+1,\sigma} c_{n,\sigma}$ | π-electron hopping between sites $n$ and $n+1$ |
| $\tfrac{1}{2}K(u_{n+1}-u_n)^2$ | Elastic (σ-skeleton) cost of bond-length change |
| $\tfrac{1}{2}M \dot{u}_n^{\,2}$ | Kinetic energy of site $n$ (treated adiabatically → set to 0) |

The *dimerised* hopping integral is
$$
t_n \;=\; t_0 \;-\; \alpha\,(u_{n+1}-u_n)
$$
where **$\alpha$ is the electron-phonon coupling constant** and $u_n$ is the displacement of site $n$.

## 2.3 Polarons, Bipolarons, Solitons — At a Glance

| Quasi-particle | Charge | Spin | Distortion | Polymer of choice |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Polaron ($P^+, P^-$)** | $\pm e$ | $1/2$ | Local bond alternation, ~4–6 sites | *cis*-PA, PPP, PPy, PANI |
| **Bipolaron ($BP^{2+}, BP^{2-}$)** | $\pm 2e$ | $0$ | Larger distortion, ~5–7 sites | Most doped conjugated polymers |
| **Soliton ($S, S^{\pm}$)** | $0$ or $\pm e$ | $1/2$ or $0$ | Topological kink (domain wall) | *trans*-polyacetylene only |
| **Polaron–exciton ($P^*$)** | $0$ | $0$ | Bond distortion + bound $e$–$h$ | Photoluminescence active polymers |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the boxed equations. They are asked verbatim in KTU ESE papers.**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{gap}^{opt} = 2\Delta_0 + 2 E_P^{bind}$ | Optical gap = Peierls gap + 2× polaron binding |
| 2 | $E_{polaron} = \dfrac{2}{\pi}\, t_0\,(0.415 - 0.02)$ | Polaron creation energy (SSH result) |
| 3 | $E_{bipolaron} = 2 E_{polaron} - E_{BB}$ | Bipolaron is stable if $E_{BB}>0$ |
| 4 | $\Delta_0 = 2\alpha\, u_0$ | Dimerisation amplitude from SSH |
| 5 | $\xi_0 = v_F / \Delta_0$ | Coherence length (soliton half-width) |
| 6 | $E_{soliton} = \dfrac{2}{\pi}\,\Delta_0$ | Soliton creation energy |
| 7 | $\hbar\Omega_0 = \sqrt{4K/M}$ | Pinning phonon frequency |
| 8 | $\sigma(\omega) \propto \delta(\omega)$ (mid-gap) | Mid-gap absorption by $S^{\pm}$ |
| 9 | $E_{hop} = E_a - \beta r$ | Inter-chain hopping (Mott–VRH) |
| 10 | $E_{Coulomb}^{e-h} = -e^2 / 4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r$ | Screened e–h Coulomb binding |

> [!WARNING]
> Note: I deliberately used `\vert` and `\mid` styling — *not* the bare pipe symbol — so the table above does not break your rendered preview. In your exam scripts, prefer `\left\vert x \right\vert` for absolute values.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OLED displays:** The polaron-exciton is the emitting species. Engineering $E_{P^*}$ tunes colour.
- **Electrochromic windows:** Bipolaron formation bleaches the π–π* band; visible colour switches with voltage.
- **Organic photovoltaic cells:** Polaron pair dissociation at donor–acceptor interfaces.
- **Biosensors:** Conducting-polymer electrodes rely on polaron-hopping conductivity.
- **Supercapacitors:** Doped polymer chains store charge as bipolarons.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dimerisation Amplitude $\Delta_0$ from Energy Minimisation

**Step 1.** Write the electronic energy per site of a half-filled band with a Peierls gap $2\Delta_0$ (standard BCS-like result for 1-D):

$$
E_{el} = -\dfrac{2}{\pi} \, t_0 \,\bigl[\sqrt{1 - (\Delta_0/t_0)^2} \;+\; (\Delta_0/t_0)\,\arcsin(\Delta_0/t_0)\bigr] \;\approx\; -\dfrac{2}{\pi}\,t_0 \,+\, \dfrac{\Delta_0^{\,2}}{\pi t_0}
$$

*Logic:* Expand the square root for small $\Delta_0/t_0$; the constant term is the undistorted band energy, and the second term is the energy gain from gap opening.

**Step 2.** Write the elastic energy per site of a dimerised chain with alternating bond length change $u_0$:

$$
E_{elast} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,K\,(2u_0)^2 \;=\; 2K u_0^{\,2}
$$

**Step 3.** Express the dimerisation gap in terms of the displacement via the SSH coupling, $\Delta_0 = 2\alpha u_0$, so $u_0 = \Delta_0 / 2\alpha$.

**Step 4.** Substitute $u_0$ into the elastic energy:

$$
E_{elast} = 2K\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta_0}{2\alpha}\right)^{\!2} \;=\; \dfrac{K}{2\alpha^2}\,\Delta_0^{\,2}
$$

**Step 5.** Total energy per site:

$$
E_{tot}(\Delta_0) \;=\; -\dfrac{2}{\pi}\,t_0 \;+\; \dfrac{\Delta_0^{\,2}}{\pi t_0} \;+\; \dfrac{K}{2\alpha^2}\,\Delta_0^{\,2}
$$

**Step 6.** Minimise with respect to $\Delta_0$: $\partial E_{tot}/\partial \Delta_0 = 0$.

$$
\dfrac{2\Delta_0}{\pi t_0} \;+\; \dfrac{K}{\alpha^2}\,\Delta_0 \;=\; 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \Delta_0\!\left(\dfrac{2}{\pi t_0} + \dfrac{K}{\alpha^2}\right) = 0
$$

The non-trivial solution is $\Delta_0 = 0$, but including the *anharmonic* elastic term $\propto u_0^4$ stabilises a finite $\Delta_0$ (Mean-field Peierls result). The **Kohmoto–Kivelson argument** gives:

$$
\Delta_0 \;\approx\; 4\,\alpha\!\left(\dfrac{t_0}{K}\right)^{\!1/2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi t_0 K}{2\alpha^2}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The non-analytic exponential dependence on $1/\alpha^2$ is the hallmark of a 1-D Peierls transition — it does **not** occur in 3-D metals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Soliton Solution of the Continuum SSH Model

**Step 1.** Take the continuum limit of $H_{SSH}$ (long-wavelength, slowly varying $u(x)$):

$$
H_{cont} = -i\hbar v_F\,(\psi^{\dagger}\sigma_3 \partial_x \psi) \;+\; \Delta(x)\,\psi^{\dagger}\sigma_1 \psi \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi\hbar v_F K'}\!\int\! dx\,\Delta^2(x)
$$

with $\Delta(x) = 2\alpha u(x)$ and Pauli matrices acting on the two-component right/left-mover spinor $\psi$.

**Step 2.** Self-consistency (gap equation):

$$
